svg.text: AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGE
NOTATION — WRITING VISUAL LANGUAGES
SIGN LANGUAGES: NO UNIVERSAL WRITING SYSTEM
Sign languages are full natural languages; not codes for spoken language
No single writing system dominates; video is the primary "text" medium
Three main notation approaches used in research and education:
SIGNWRITING (SUTTON 1974)
SignWriting: visual symbols for handshapes, movement, location, expression
Written top-to-bottom; handshape symbols + movement arrows
Used in Deaf education in some countries (Nicaragua, Denmark, Brazil)
HAMNOSYS AND STOKOE NOTATION
HamNoSys: Hamburg Notation System; used in linguistic research
Stokoe notation (1960): first formal sign language analysis system
William Stokoe: proved ASL is a complete language, not "signed English"
GLOSSING: PRACTICAL NOTATION
Glossing: write English word for each sign in caps (IX-1 = "I/me")
WOMAN GIVE BOOK IX-3 = "The woman gives him/her a book"
Gloss shows meaning but not form; used by interpreters and researchers
LSM · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
LENGUA DE SENAS MEXICANA (LSM) NOTATION
LSM: same notation systems as ASL (SignWriting, glossing, HamNoSys)
LSM glosses use Spanish words: MUJER DA LIBRO = "The woman gives a book"
LSM has its own distinct signs; not same as ASL (different languages)
FINGERSPELLING: MANUAL ALPHABETS
ASL: one-handed manual alphabet (26 handshapes for A-Z)
LSM: uses same one-handed alphabet as ASL (shared with Spanish)
BSL: two-handed manual alphabet (different from ASL!)
NOTATION COMPARISON
Both: no standard written form for everyday use
Both: fingerspelling for proper nouns and borrowed words
Both: video recordings now serve as the primary "text" medium
Sign languages: oral languages with no writing = normal; not a deficit
PLATE 62a ALT · ASL / LSM · NOTATION
